## About Nesta

Nesta is an innovation foundation. For us, innovation means turning bold ideas into reality and changing
lives for the better. We use our expertise, skills and funding in areas where there are big challenges facing society.

# Clarifying cultural policy and creative industries policy: the role of statistics

The time has come for the UK Government to classify and publish stats on the cultural sector alongside its Creative Industries Economic Estimates.

![Hasan Bakhshi](https://media.nesta.org.uk/images/hasan_bakhshi.2e16d0ba.fill-112x112.jpg)

## Hasan Bakhshi

### Hasan Bakhshi

Director, Creative Industries Policy and Evidence Centre

Hasan oversaw Nesta's creative economy policy, research and practical work.

## Clarifying cultural policy and creative industries policy: the role of statistics

Twitter

Facebook

Linkedin

Bluesky

In the creative industries, classification and measurement over almost two decades has contributed to healthy – albeit fraught – debates and better outcomes on a range of topics central to public policy.

For example, the ability to measure the relationship between creativity and information and communications technologies (ICT) has given rise to distinctively UK initiatives like*[Next Gen](/node/137)*and ICT curriculum reform in English schools.

The difficulties in capturing crafts in official classifications has led to rigorous data and measurement underpinning the role of the crafts in creativity which has given it a renewed voice in current debates about creativity and the future of work. And developing the statistical evidence for the distinction between the creative industries and wider creative economy has shown that the economic stakes for the UK are much higher than the fate of individual industries.

In our new provocation,*[Cultural Policy in the Time of the Creative Industries](http://www.nesta.org.uk/publications/cultural-policy-time-creative-industries)*, Stuart Cunningham and I argue that the time has come for the UK government to classify and publish statistics on the cultural sector (those industries primarily involved in the mass production, circulation and consumption of symbolic texts according to one influential definition) alongside its existing highly regarded [Creative Industries Economic Estimates](https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/creative-industries-economic-estimates-january-2016).

What is needed are official statistics which allow both cultural and creative activity to be taken full account of, acknowledging both the substantial overlap between the two areas of activity, as well as dealing as best as possible with the well-known challenges that underestimate the extent of cultural activity (for example, the extent of voluntary activity).

Our prediction is that if government begins to classify and publish statistics on the cultural sector and creative industries this way, it will lead to better and more transparent cultural and creative industries policy. It would bring to greater prominence such matters as the scope of the cultural sector as recognised by government; who cultural policy is being made for, and who is making it; what counts as cultural participation, and the importance of cultural economy vs cultural industries.

It would also help to clarify that the cultural sector is supported by government mainly on cultural grounds, and that the creative industries are supported primarily because of their economic contribution. Both the cultural and economic wellbeing of the nation are of paramount importance to government.

As a prominent actor in this space, the OECD has a mandate to support the G20 Working Group on Culture to build “a strong evidence base on the economic footprint of the sector and its contribution to economies and societies, so as to inform policy action at international, national and local levels.” (G20 Culture Working Group Terms of Reference, 2023). In addition, the OECD Summer Academy for Cultural and Creative Industries and Local Development (SACCI), run from the OECD Trento Centre, Italy, equips policy makers and practitioners with the tools to design forward-thinking policies that respond to the needs of the sector.

# Cultural and creative sectors

We provide evidence and guidance to countries, cities and regions on ways to maximise the economic and social impact of culture and support the creative economy.

Focus

## Key links

[OECD-ICOM Guide for Local Governments, Communities and Museums](/en/publications/culture-and-local-development-maximising-the-impact_9a855be5-en.html)

Share

[Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Ftopics%2Fsub-issues%2Fculture-creative-industries-and-sports%2Fcultural-and-creative-sectors.html)
[Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Ftopics%2Fsub-issues%2Fculture-creative-industries-and-sports%2Fcultural-and-creative-sectors.html)
[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Ftopics%2Fsub-issues%2Fculture-creative-industries-and-sports%2Fcultural-and-creative-sectors.html)

## About cultural and creative sectors

**Cultural and creative sectors are an often-overlooked driver of development; generating economic wealth, creating jobs, and spurring innovation, as well as making places more attractive to live, work and invest in.**

Moreover, there is growing evidence that increased levels of cultural participation have positive effects on well-being and health, as well as encouraging social cohesion by supporting the integration and inclusion of marginalised groups.

## Our mission

The OECD helps governments to:

* Mainstream culture beyond cultural policies and as an integral part of wider policy agendas, such as employment, social cohesion, innovation, health and well-being, the environment and sustainable local development.
* Adapt policies on employment, social protection, innovation and entrepreneurship to the needs of cultural and creative sectors.
* Improve internationally comparable statistics and the evidence base on the scale, scope and impacts of culture and creative sectors.
* Build capacity of national and local governments to integrate culture and into broader economic and social development strategies.
* Leverage opportunities such as large-scale cultural, sporting and business events as catalysts for local development.

## How it works

### 

The OECD works with national and local authorities to help shape evidence-based policies to address the needs of cultural and creative sectors. Each review provides tailored assessment of the policy landscape for cultural and creative sectors, as well as evidence on CCS economic trends and footprint in the review location, situated within national and international benchmarks. The review series also works to highlight good practice initiatives at an international level and to provide recommendations to local and national governments on how to best support the cultural and creative ecosystem in their location.

### 

The OECD works to help close data gaps for CCS, to better inform policies at national and local levels and channel investment. This includes harmonising CCS statistics across the OECD, working closely with national statistics offices to build capabilities in CCS data collection and analysis, and serving as a forum for advancing data collection on CCS at the international level. To support this work, our informal group of experts on CCS data and measurement approaches meets twice a year to exchange on recent developments related to national and international CCS statistical frameworks and to offer feedback on OECD projects.

### 

The OECD supports the G20 Culture Working Group with [background analysis](https://www.oecd.org/cfe/leed/OECD-G20-Culture-July-2021.pdf) and advice, as well as supporting the G20 Tourism Working Group, with [inputs relating to cultural tourism](https://www.oecd.org/cfe/leed/OECD-G20-TWG-Discussion-Paper-Tourism-Cultural-Creative-Sectors.pdf). As a prominent actor in this space, the OECD has a mandate to support the G20 Working Group on Culture to build “a strong evidence base on the economic footprint of the sector and its contribution to economies and societies, so as to inform policy action at international, national and local levels.” ([G20 Culture Working Group Terms of Reference, 2023](https://g7g20-documents.org/database/document/2023-g20-india-sherpa-track-culture-ministers-ministers-annex-g20-culture-ministers-meeting-annex-terms-of-reference#section-1)). The OECD also works closely with other international organisations, such as UNESCO, EUROSTAT, the World Bank, and the ILO to help shape the global agenda for cultural and creative sectors.

### 

We provide guidance and toolkits on a wide range of issues relating to cultural and creative sectors.

For example, the [OECD-ICOM Guide for Local Governments, Communities and Museums](/en/about/projects/museums-and-local-development.html) is a learning, self-assessment and development tool to inform and support policy makers and the museum community in maximising the impact of culture and heritage on local development. The toolkit can be used by local and regional governments to assess and improve their approaches to maximising the social and economic value of cultural heritage, by museums to assess and strengthen their existing and potential linkages with the local economy and social fabric, and as a learning tool for everybody interested in culture and heritage as lever for local development.

Find out more about how the OECD-ICOM project on museums and local development, including case studies, events, and free downloads of the Guide in English, French, Greek, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Polish, Portuguese, and Spanish.

### 

The OECD organises regular conferences, workshops, and expert meetings where policy makers, industry stakeholders, and experts from around the world come together to exchange knowledge, discuss challenges, and explore opportunities for better cultural and creative sectors policy.

In addition, the [OECD Summer Academy for Cultural and Creative Industries and Local Development (SACCI)](/en/networks/summer-academy-on-cultural-and-creative-industries-and-local-development.html), run from the [OECD Trento Centre](/en/about/programmes/trento-centre.html), Italy, equips policy makers and practitioners with the tools to design forward-thinking policies that respond to the needs of the sector. Each year, the Summer Academy tackles a different theme, and invites policy makers and practitioners from around the world to share their experiences and learn from international experts in the field.

## New projects

Project

# Boosting innovation and productivity through cultural and creative sectors

CCS are highly innovative, producing new products, services and content; developing new business models and ways of working; and developing and integrating technologies in novel ways. They also contribute to innovation in other sectors of the economy through the services they provide (such as design and advertising), and as a source of new ideas.

[Read more](/content/dam/oecd/en/topics/policy-sub-issues/culture,-creative-industries-and-sports/pdf/OECD-Project-Boosting-innovation-and-productivity-through-cultural-and-creative-sectors.pdf/_jcr_content/renditions/original./OECD-Project-Boosting-innovation-and-productivity-through-cultural-and-creative-sectors.pdf)

Project

# Space for culture

Cultural and creative spaces, such as museums, makers studios and music venues, add vibrancy to a place, contribute to city branding, and can help revitalise city centres and main streets. In turn, the availability of space is critical for creative workers and firms to earn a living and run their business, while poor access to cultural amenities can be a large barrier to cultural engagement, particularly for low-income groups. However, making space for culture is not easy.

[Read more](/content/dam/oecd/en/topics/policy-sub-issues/culture,-creative-industries-and-sports/pdf/OECD-project-Space-for-Culture.pdf/_jcr_content/renditions/original./OECD-project-Space-for-Culture.pdf)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cultural policy is vital for fostering creativity, ensuring cultural diversity, and enhancing economic growth, as highlighted by UNESCO's focus on cultural expressions in the creative economy.
A balanced approach to cultural policy integrates economic goals with cultural sustainability, ensuring that both sectors thrive without compromising artistic integrity or public access.
Active engagement of stakeholders—including artists, policymakers, and communities—is essential for effective cultural policy implementation and fostering a vibrant and inclusive creative economy.
